source_item.title: It's a revamp! RBI steps ahead on plan for plastic rupee notes

slide1_hook.label: India's Paper Rupee Era May Be Over
slide1_hook.subhead: RBI takes its first real procurement step toward plastic money.

slide2_backstory.label: THE BACKSTORY
slide2_backstory.headline: India Tried This Before, and Quit
slide2_backstory.body: In 2012, India tested a billion plastic ₹10 notes in five cities. It got shelved because ATMs couldn't handle the thicker notes.

slide3_news.label: THE UPDATE
slide3_news.headline: RBI's Print Arm Is Calling Suppliers
slide3_news.body: RBI's printing arm just asked suppliers to bid by August 18 for polymer notes. ₹10 and ₹20 notes are the likely first candidates, since they wear out fastest.

slide4_why.label: WHY IT MATTERS
slide4_why.headline: Cheaper, Tougher, Harder to Fake
bullets: 23.8 billion worn notes destroyed in FY25 alone
Plastic notes last 2.5x longer than paper
60+ countries use them, and they're hard to fake

slide5_engage.label: YOUR TAKE
slide5_engage.headline: India still runs on cash, with currency at 12.1% of GDP. But if your ₹10 note can survive a wash, would that change how you use money?
slide5_engage.cta: Follow for more on how India's economy is quietly changing, one policy at a time.
